description: 15-question policy quiz template for Senate and House races. 2 questions per major issue area covering both a broad position and a specific policy mechanism. All questions use Likert scale: Strongly Oppose (1) → Strongly Support (5). Run through bias audit pipeline before approving.
note: When a voter marks an issue area as a priority in the baseline quiz, questions tagged with that issueKey receive a 2.5x weight multiplier in match score computation.

questions.questionText: Would you support raising the federal minimum wage to $17 per hour?
questions.rationale: Tests broad economic floor position. Clear policy number reduces ambiguity.

questions.questionText: Would you support imposing tariffs of 25% or more on imports from countries that do not meet US labor and environmental standards?
questions.rationale: Tests trade protectionism with neutral framing — grounds tariffs in labor/environment rather than nationalism.
questions.auditNote: Watch for loaded language around 'protecting American workers' vs 'raising consumer prices'. Both framings bias the question.

questions.questionText: Would you support expanding Medicare to cover all Americans regardless of age?
questions.rationale: Broad coverage question. 'Medicare for All' label avoided — policy described instead.

questions.questionText: Would you support federal legislation requiring Medicare to negotiate prescription drug prices directly with pharmaceutical manufacturers?
questions.rationale: Specific mechanism question. High voter salience, strong candidate differentiation on this.

questions.questionText: Would you support legislation requiring the US to reduce carbon emissions by 50% below 2005 levels by 2035?
questions.rationale: Specific target and baseline year make this unambiguous. Avoids 'Green New Deal' label.

questions.questionText: Would you support ending new federal permits for oil and gas drilling on public lands and offshore waters?
questions.rationale: Specific mechanism. 'New permits' framing is precise — avoids implying immediate shutdown of existing operations.
questions.auditNote: Audit for asymmetry — 'ending permits' sounds restrictive. Ensure Likert scale framing is genuinely neutral.

questions.questionText: Would you support a pathway to citizenship for undocumented immigrants who have lived and worked continuously in the US for more than 5 years?
questions.rationale: 'Continuously lived and worked' sets a specific condition that avoids blanket amnesty framing.

questions.questionText: Would you support significantly increasing federal funding for border security infrastructure and personnel at the US southern border?
questions.rationale: Tests the other axis of immigration debate. 'Infrastructure and personnel' is neutral — doesn't specify wall vs. technology.
questions.auditNote: Watch for 'significantly' loading the question. Consider 'substantially increasing' or a specific dollar figure if candidate data supports it.

questions.questionText: Would you support requiring background checks for all firearm purchases in the US, including private sales and gun show transactions?
questions.rationale: 'Including private sales and gun shows' closes the loophole specifically — makes the question policy-precise.

questions.questionText: Would you support a federal ban on the manufacture and sale of semi-automatic rifles with detachable magazines to civilian buyers?
questions.rationale: Technical definition avoids 'assault weapon' label. 'Civilian buyers' excludes law enforcement — reduces false framing.
questions.auditNote: 'Ban' is a loaded word in some framings. Consider 'restrict the manufacture and sale of' as a lower-bias alternative.

questions.questionText: Would you support raising the top federal income tax rate to 50% for individual income exceeding $10 million per year?
questions.rationale: Specific threshold ($10M) avoids 'tax the rich' framing. Most voters don't reach this bracket — reduces personal threat response.

questions.questionText: Would you support raising the federal corporate income tax rate from 21% to 28%?
questions.rationale: Specific numbers (21% → 28%) anchor the question in fact. Avoids 'making corporations pay their fair share' framing.

questions.questionText: Would you support increasing the federal defense budget by more than 5% per year above the rate of inflation?
questions.rationale: 'Above inflation' is the meaningful threshold — without it, any increase sounds modest.

questions.questionText: Would you support maintaining or increasing current levels of US foreign assistance to allied and developing nations?
questions.rationale: Combines maintenance and increase to give a directional signal. 'Allied and developing' is broader than 'foreign aid' framing.
questions.auditNote: Watch for anti-aid framing ('giving money overseas while Americans suffer'). Question must stay descriptive.

questions.questionText: Would you support federal legislation requiring all states to automatically register eligible citizens to vote when they interact with government agencies?
questions.rationale: 'When they interact with government agencies' is more precise than 'at age 18' — reflects how AVR actually works.
